albums.title: The Inevitable End
albums.reason: With over 1,000 all-time plays, Röyksopp are one of your most cherished artists, and this 2014 swan song — their final album as a traditional release — is a gorgeous, bittersweet record that deserves a proper revisit.

albums.title: Pocket Symphony
albums.reason: Air sit at over 1,000 all-time plays for you, and this quieter, string-laden 2007 album is the one that often gets forgotten — it's ripe for rediscovery.

albums.title: 100th Window
albums.reason: Massive Attack have 534 all-time plays and you know Mezzanine and Blue Lines well — this darker, more introspective 2003 record is the one in their catalogue most likely to feel fresh again.

albums.title: Blue Album
albums.reason: Orbital clock in at 777 all-time plays, and this underrated 2004 comeback album has some of their warmest, most emotional work — well worth dusting off.

albums.title: Surrender
albums.reason: With 556 all-time plays, The Chemical Brothers are deep in your DNA, and Surrender — home to Hey Boy Hey Girl and The Sunshine Underground — is a stone-cold classic from their imperial peak.
Moby sits at 551 all-time plays for you, and while Play is the obvious choice, this 2002 follow-up is equally beautiful and far less revisited — a hidden gem in plain sight.

albums.title: Outlier
albums.reason: Bonobo have 440 all-time plays and feature in your recent 12 months — Outlier from 2023 is his most recent and most introspective album, a collection of quieter, more experimental pieces that reward close listening.

albums.title: MORE D4TA
albums.reason: Moderat appear in your top tracks with Rusty Nails, and MORE D4TA from 2022 is their most recent record — sharper and more dancefloor-focused than their earlier work, but unmistakably them.

albums.title: Assume Form
albums.reason: James Blake is your most-played individual track artist with Postpone at 156 plays, and Assume Form from 2019 is his most open-hearted and sonically lush record — a beautiful place to revisit his work from.

albums.title: Into the Trees
albums.reason: Trentemøller has 560 all-time plays and features in your top tracks — this 2010 album is a deeper, more gothic journey than The Last Resort and rewards patient listening.

albums.title: New Energy
albums.reason: Four Tet features in your recent 12 months with 30 plays and New Energy sits in your recent albums list — this 2017 record is his most optimistic and emotionally rich work, and it suits where your taste has been heading.

albums.title: Love Hope
albums.reason: Maribou State have 434 all-time plays and Beginner's Luck is in your top tracks — Love Hope from 2024 is their most recent and emotionally mature album, a natural fit for where your taste sits right now.

albums.title: Kiasmos
albums.reason: If Ólafur Arnalds' classical side and the driving pulse of Orbital's best work were fused into one project, you'd get Kiasmos — this self-titled debut is stunningly restrained and deeply emotional.

albums.title: Mean Love
albums.reason: Your love of Stevie Wonder, Marvin Gaye and the funkier end of Daft Punk makes Sinkane a natural fit — Mean Love blends Afrobeat, soul and post-punk into something genuinely joyful and original.

albums.title: Principe del Norte
albums.reason: Your deep love of Air, Röyksopp and the Scandinavian electronic tradition points directly at Prins Thomas — this sprawling 2016 record is warm, cosmic and unhurried in exactly the way you seem to love.

albums.title: Wading
albums.reason: Drawing on the same jazz-meets-electronics spirit that runs through your love of Plaid and Aphex Twin, Photay's 2017 debut is intricate, playful and full of musical ideas you haven't heard arranged quite like this.

albums.title: Planets
albums.reason: If you love the quiet intimacy of Röyksopp's slower moments and the textural depth of Pink Floyd, this Japanese duo's 2004 album sits beautifully in that space — underseen and completely gorgeous.

albums.title: The English Riviera
albums.reason: Your love of The Strokes, The Shins and the poppier side of electronic music makes Metronomy's breakthrough 2011 album a natural discovery — witty, melodically rich and unlike anything else from that moment.

albums.title: Ondes
albums.reason: Your recent immersion in Sault, jazz-adjacent sounds and soul suggests you'd love this Paris-based saxophonist whose 2022 album blends spiritual jazz, Afrobeat and deep electronic production.

albums.title: Come Back to the Light
albums.reason: Your recent pull toward meditative, emotionally open music — Nils Frahm, Brambles, Ben Lukas Boysen — aligns perfectly with this 2024 record, which is gentle, wise and quietly life-affirming.

albums.title: Ill Considered 8
albums.reason: Given your recent love of Portico Quartet, Kamaal Williams and jazz-influenced electronic music, this London quartet's raw, spiritual free-jazz sessions feel like a natural next step into something more urgent and unguarded.

albums.title: Where Future Unfolds
albums.reason: Your recent listening to Sault, Charles Bradley and Curtis Mayfield shows a pull toward music with spiritual weight and community — this 2019 album channels that same energy through jazz, gospel and electronics.

albums.title: Chicos de Nazca
albums.reason: Your recent love of Hermanos Gutiérrez's dusty, meditative guitar work points toward this Colombian duo, whose self-titled debut takes a similar devotional approach to instrumental music with a deeper South American mysticism.

albums.title: Cartography
albums.reason: Your recent listening to Hania Rani, Ólafur Arnalds and Nils Frahm suggests a genuine appetite for music that sits at the edge of jazz and contemporary classical — this Norwegian trumpeter's 2008 album is sublimely quiet and searching.

albums.title: Memory Streams
albums.reason: Portico Quartet are your second most-played artist this month and Ruins is on heavy rotation — Memory Streams from 2022 is their most recent and most electronically expansive album, a natural next destination from where you are right now.

albums.title: Cascade
albums.reason: Ben Lukas Boysen is all over your last 7 days with Golden Times 1 on repeat — Cascade is his most cinematic and orchestrally rich album, carrying the same patient, layered beauty you're clearly craving this week.

albums.title: The Universe Smiles Upon You II
albums.reason: Khruangbin are your most-played artist this month across multiple albums — this 2023 companion piece to their debut revisits their warmest, most meditative instrumental mode, and feels exactly right for where you are this week.

albums.title: Traces
albums.reason: Parra for Cuva appeared in your listening just this week — Traces is his debut album, full of the same shimmering, emotionally transparent electronic music as the track you just played.

albums.title: Mind Bokeh
albums.reason: Bibio showed up in your last 7 days via Lovers' Carvings — Mind Bokeh is his most expansive and electric record, stretching from the folksy intimacy you know into something more psychedelic and groove-driven.

albums.title: Suddenly
albums.reason: You played Caribou's Sun just this week — Suddenly is his most emotionally raw album, built around personal loss and looping micro-samples, and it carries the same aching introspective quality as so much of what you're playing right now.

djSets.setName: Fred again.. | Floating Points | Caribou - Lyon Live stream 24th october
Max Cooper Live at Das Bett, Frankfurt (8 March 25)
